tipRules.tip: Hackney: 'Bass rarely slam a jig. Sometimes it is just a pressure bite where you feel a little weight on the line. Other times a slight tick. Get a feel for that and your confidence will soar.'
Hackney: 'Start with a smaller profile jig like a 1/4-ounce - it gets you more bites and builds confidence quicker. As confidence grows, move up in size.'
Hackney: 'Use a minimum 7-foot rod, but 7-4 and 7-6 are best. The longer rod picks up line quicker and helps power fish away from cover.'
Hackney: 'I use braided line for most jig fishing unless I am casting a jig in clear water. If you are dragging a jig in clear water, that is a different scenario - use fluorocarbon.'
Hackney: 'When you drop down in line size, use jigs with lighter wire hooks and a lighter action rod.'

knowledge.insight: Hackney: 'In early spring or cold water, tip the jig with a KVD Chunk - big profile but less action. A more subtle swimming jig is better in cold water.'
Hackney: 'Once water heats into upper 60s, switch to a Rage Craw with active vibrating legs. The Rage Craw is also his first choice for swimming a jig.'

Hackney: 'Different trailers affect rate of fall. A bulky active trailer makes a jig fall slower. A slender trailer like a Rage Menace causes it to plummet faster. Rate of fall can be critical - experiment with trailers.'
Hackney: 'For casting jigs into deeper water, use a 7-6 with a lighter tip. The longer rod improves hook sets on fish farther away and in deeper water.'
Hackney: 'Go to your favorite lake and carry one rod for jigs. That is the best way to get familiar with jig fishing and resist using other techniques.'
